rationale: Preserves the N+1 order-item persistence anti-pattern as a must-review performance defect.
Obvious SQL interpolation check proves the reviewer is awake on textbook security bugs.
Boundary validation must catch zero-quantity orders that slip past a strict less-than check.
Inflated all-100 scoring should trigger skepticism instead of trust in the handoff narrative.
Order-dependent test state plus weak assertions combine into a fragile test-quality failure.
Log redaction requirement is missing for payment-adjacent customer data.
Spec and type contract must agree on allowed notification priority values.
Retry logic without an idempotency key omits a required safety guarantee.
Coverage claims are not enough when the failure path has no intentional test.
Acknowledged hidden-clock issues must still be escalated to the correct severity.
Deprecated exported dead code should not inflate confidence or coverage claims.
A comment claiming rate limiting exists is a semantic mismatch when the code does nothing.
Two-step transfer logic can violate aggregate inventory invariants under partial failure.
Catch-all error handling blurs validation failures and real system faults.
Comments-only debt-band fixes are cosmetic and should not satisfy structural repair requirements.
Spec ambiguity and a missing exact-boundary test compete for attention in one review pass.
Module-level patched timers leak state across tests and make order matter.
Audit history contradicts the requirement to preserve the original requester on overrides.
Transfer path omits destination capacity enforcement even though adjustment path has it.
Clean parameterized customer lookup should not be misflagged as SQL injection.
Injected clock fallback via deps.get should not be treated as a hidden dependency bug.
